C:
Build on Postgres. For an early B2B SaaS startup, it's the lower-risk, higher-leverage default, and the cases where MongoDB wins rarely apply to you yet.

Here's the reasoning.

**B2B SaaS data is relational, and you'll know it the moment you bill someone.** Organizations have users, users have roles, subscriptions have line items, resources belong to workspaces with permission grids. That's a web of foreign-key relationships with hard integrity requirements — you cannot have an invoice pointing at a deleted account. Postgres enforces this for you with foreign keys, constraints, and real multi-row transactions. In MongoDB you either denormalize (and then fight update anomalies across duplicated copies) or you reimplement joins and referential integrity in application code, which is exactly the bug surface you don't want a three-person team owning.

**You don't yet know your access patterns, and Postgres punishes that ignorance less.** MongoDB's document model rewards you when you know the queries up front and can shape documents around them. Early on you're changing the product weekly — new filters, new reports, a sales team asking "can you pull all accounts in healthcare that churned last quarter." Ad-hoc relational queries and `JOIN`s are Postgres's home turf; the same questions against a denormalized document store often mean a schema migration or an aggregation pipeline. Postgres lets you defer the modeling decisions you're not ready to make.

**Schema flexibility — the historical reason to pick Mongo — is now a Postgres feature.** `JSONB` columns give you schemaless storage with indexing (GIN), and you can mix a rigid relational core (accounts, billing, auth) with flexible JSON for the genuinely variable parts (per-tenant settings, event payloads, feature flags). You get document flexibility where you want it without giving up integrity everywhere else.

**It scales further than an early startup will reach for years.** A single well-indexed Postgres instance handles tens of thousands of tenants and very large tables before you need read replicas or partitioning. The "Mongo scales, Postgres doesn't" framing is about horizontal write sharding at a scale most B2B SaaS companies never hit, and if you do, you'll have the team and money to handle it deliberately.

**Operational gravity favors Postgres.** Managed Postgres is everywhere and cheap (RDS/Aurora, Cloud SQL, Supabase, Neon, Crunchy). The hiring pool knows SQL. ORMs, analytics tools, BI dashboards, and your eventual data warehouse all assume Postgres-shaped data. That ecosystem friction compounds quietly in your favor.

**When Mongo would actually be the better call** — and these are worth being honest about: your core data is genuinely document-shaped and hierarchical (a CMS, a flexible form builder, deeply nested per-document content), write patterns are append-heavy with few cross-entity relationships, or you have a concrete near-term need for horizontal write scaling you can already see. If none of those describe you — and for most B2B SaaS they don't — Postgres is the right primary store.

My recommendation: start on Postgres, use `JSONB` for the parts that are legitimately fluid, and add specialized stores (Redis for cache, a search engine, a document store) later only when a specific need proves itself. Reach for one default that does the relational core well, and keep the rest of the stack boring.
T:
Build on Postgres. For an early B2B SaaS startup, it's the lower-risk, higher-leverage default, and the cases where MongoDB wins rarely apply to you yet.

Here's the reasoning.

**B2B SaaS data is relational, and you'll know it the moment you bill someone.** Organizations have users, users have roles, subscriptions have line items, resources belong to workspaces with permission grids. That's a web of foreign-key relationships with hard integrity requirements — you cannot have an invoice pointing at a deleted account. Postgres enforces this for you with foreign keys, constraints, and real multi-row transactions. In MongoDB you either denormalize (and then fight update anomalies across duplicated copies) or you reimplement joins and referential integrity in application code, which is exactly the bug surface you don't want a three-person team owning.

**You don't yet know your access patterns, and Postgres punishes that ignorance less.** MongoDB's document model rewards you when you know the queries up front and can shape documents around them. Early on you're changing the product weekly — new filters, new reports, a sales team asking "can you pull all accounts in healthcare that churned last quarter." Ad-hoc relational queries and `JOIN`s are Postgres's home turf; the same questions against a denormalized document store often mean a schema migration or an aggregation pipeline. Postgres lets you defer the modeling decisions you're not ready to make.

**Schema flexibility — the historical reason to pick Mongo — is now a Postgres feature.** `JSONB` columns give you schemaless storage with indexing (GIN), and you can mix a rigid relational core (accounts, billing, auth) with flexible JSON for the genuinely variable parts (per-tenant settings, event payloads, feature flags). You get document flexibility where you want it without giving up integrity everywhere else.

**It scales further than an early startup will reach for years.** A single well-indexed Postgres instance handles tens of thousands of tenants and very large tables before you need read replicas or partitioning. The "Mongo scales, Postgres doesn't" framing is about horizontal write sharding at a scale most B2B SaaS companies never hit, and if you do, you'll have the team and money to handle it deliberately.

**Operational gravity favors Postgres.** Managed Postgres is everywhere and cheap (RDS/Aurora, Cloud SQL, Supabase, Neon, Crunchy). The hiring pool knows SQL. ORMs, analytics tools, BI dashboards, and your eventual data warehouse all assume Postgres-shaped data. That ecosystem friction compounds quietly in your favor.

**When Mongo would actually be the better call** — and these are worth being honest about: your core data is genuinely document-shaped and hierarchical (a CMS, a flexible form builder, deeply nested per-document content), write patterns are append-heavy with few cross-entity relationships, or you have a concrete near-term need for horizontal write scaling you can already see. If none of those describe you — and for most B2B SaaS they don't — Postgres is the right primary store.

**What would change my answer.** This recommendation rests on one assumption: that your core domain is relational and your scale is ordinary early-startup scale. So the disconfirmers are concrete. If you can already sketch your primary entities and they're genuinely a tree of self-similar, deeply nested documents that you almost always read and write as one whole unit — not a graph of cross-referencing tables — the document model stops being a liability and Mongo becomes a real contender. Likewise if you have a *demonstrated* (not hypothetical) need for horizontal write scaling or massive ingest from day one, or if your team has deep MongoDB operational experience and zero SQL fluency, the balance shifts. The honest limitation: I'm reasoning from the typical shape of B2B SaaS, not from your actual schema or load numbers. If your real access patterns contradict the relational assumption above, weight that evidence over this default — and note that the failure mode here is recoverable either way (Postgres `JSONB` buys you document flexibility without committing, which is part of why it's the safer place to be wrong).

My recommendation: start on Postgres, use `JSONB` for the parts that are legitimately fluid, and add specialized stores (Redis for cache, a search engine, a document store) later only when a specific need proves itself. Reach for one default that does the relational core well, and keep the rest of the stack boring.
P:
**Build on Postgres.** For an early B2B SaaS startup it's the lower-risk, higher-leverage default, and the situations where MongoDB genuinely wins rarely describe a company at your stage.

Here's the reasoning.

**Your data is relational, and you'll feel it the first time you bill someone.** B2B SaaS is organizations containing users, users holding roles, subscriptions carrying line items, resources scoped to workspaces behind permission grids — a web of foreign-key relationships with hard integrity rules. An invoice must never point at a deleted account. Postgres enforces that for you with foreign keys, constraints, and transactions that span the several rows a single business action touches. MongoDB has multi-document transactions too (since 4.0), but they cut against the grain of the document model and carry performance caveats — the model's own advice is to denormalize instead, and then you're either fighting update anomalies across duplicated copies or reimplementing joins and referential integrity in application code. That's exactly the bug surface you don't want a three-person team owning.

**You don't yet know your access patterns, and Postgres punishes that ignorance less.** The document model pays off when you know your queries up front and can shape documents around them. Early on you're changing the product weekly — a new filter, a new report, a salesperson asking "pull every healthcare account that churned last quarter." Ad-hoc queries and joins are Postgres's home turf; the same questions against a denormalized document store often mean a migration or a hand-built aggregation pipeline. Postgres lets you defer the modeling decisions you're not ready to make.

**Schema flexibility — the classic reason to reach for Mongo — is now a Postgres feature.** `JSONB` columns give you schemaless storage with real indexing (GIN), so you keep a rigid relational core (accounts, billing, auth) and drop flexible JSON exactly where the data is genuinely variable (per-tenant settings, event payloads, feature flags). Document flexibility where you want it, integrity everywhere else.

**It scales well past where you'll reach for years.** One well-indexed Postgres instance serves tens of thousands of tenants and very large tables before you need read replicas or partitioning. The "Mongo scales, Postgres doesn't" story is really about horizontal write sharding — a problem most B2B SaaS never hits, and if you do, you'll have the team and revenue to solve it deliberately rather than pre-paying for it now.

**The ecosystem compounds in your favor.** Managed Postgres is everywhere and cheap (RDS/Aurora, Cloud SQL, Supabase, Neon, Crunchy). Every engineer you hire already knows SQL. Your ORM, your BI dashboards, and your eventual data warehouse all assume Postgres-shaped data. That absence of friction adds up quietly over every quarter.

**Where Mongo would actually be the right call.** The honest exceptions: your core entity is genuinely document-shaped and deeply nested (a CMS, a flexible form builder, per-document content trees); your writes are append-heavy with few cross-entity relationships; or you have a concrete, near-term horizontal-write-scaling need you can already see coming. If none of those fit — and for most B2B SaaS none do — Postgres is your primary store.

**Bottom line:** start on Postgres, reach for `JSONB` on the parts that are legitimately fluid, and add specialized stores (Redis for cache, a search engine, a dedicated document or analytics store) later — each only once a specific need has proven itself. One default that nails the relational core, and keep the rest of the stack boring.
